metadata.summary: Design systems that maintain availability targets, recover from failures, and degrade gracefully under stress through SLO/SLI definitions, fault tolerance patterns, and disaster recovery strategies.

content.title: Reliability Architecture
content.summary: Designing systems that maintain availability targets, recover from failures, and degrade gracefully under stress. Core outputs: SLO/SLI/error-budget definitions, fault-tolerance pattern selection (circuit breaker, retry with jitter, bulkhead, timeout), graceful degradation tiers, health check endpoints, chaos engineering plan, and DR strategy (RPO/RTO, 3-2-1 backups).

why.p: Without explicit reliability design, every component failure becomes a user-facing incident. Error budgets translate business uptime goals (99.9% = 43 min/month downtime) into actionable engineering constraints. Patterns like circuit breakers prevent cascading failures; bulkheads stop one failing dependency from exhausting shared resources.

list.item: Defining SLOs before a new service goes to production
Conducting an architecture review where availability targets exceed 99.9%
Adding fault tolerance after an outage revealed cascading failure modes
Designing health check endpoints for Kubernetes probes
Planning a chaos engineering programme or DR drill
MVP or internal tool where 99% availability (3.65 days/year downtime) is acceptable — over-engineering adds cost
Single-service monolith without external dependencies — most patterns target distributed call paths
When the bottleneck is a business/product problem, not an infrastructure one

section.name: slo-sli-and-error-budget
section.p: Defines the measurement vocabulary (SLI), targets (SLO), and engineering budget (error budget) that translate business uptime goals into actionable constraints.

rule.statement: Choose SLIs that measure user-visible behaviour, not internal component health.
rule.rationale: Internal CPU metrics do not tell you whether users are experiencing failures. Measuring from the user perspective (successful requests / total requests) ensures the SLO reflects real service quality.

rule.statement: Set SLO targets slightly stricter than SLA to preserve an internal buffer before contractual breach.
rule.rationale: If SLA = 99.9%, set SLO = 99.95%. The gap is the operational buffer for undetected degradation.

section.name: sli-types-by-service
section.p: Common SLIs mapped to service categories:

list.item: API: request latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate (5xx / total), throughput
Database: query latency, availability, replication lag
Storage: durability (data loss probability), read/write latency
Streaming: end-to-end latency, message loss rate, consumer lag
Batch: success rate, processing duration, data freshness

section.name: availability-tiers
section.p: Map business context to availability target before choosing patterns:

list.item: 99% (2 nines): 3.65 days/year downtime — internal tools, dev environments
99.9% (3 nines): 8.76 hours/year — business applications, growth-stage products
99.99% (4 nines): 52.6 minutes/year — critical services, e-commerce checkout
99.999% (5 nines): 5.26 minutes/year — financial, healthcare, safety-critical

rule.statement: Calculate monthly error budget as (1 - SLO) * 43,200 minutes and enforce a feature freeze when it is exhausted.
rule.rationale: Error budget converts an abstract percentage into a concrete time allowance that engineering and product can negotiate. Exhausted budget means reliability work takes priority over features.

example.title: error-budget-calculation
example.note: SLO = 99.95% → error budget = 0.05% * 43,200 min = 21.6 min/month. Alert at 50% consumed (10.8 min used) to trigger reliability review before breach.

rule.statement: Use rolling 30-day windows for error budget calculation, not calendar months.
rule.rationale: Rolling windows avoid cliff effects where budget resets at month boundary, hiding sustained slow degradation.

section.name: fault-tolerance-patterns
section.p: Circuit Breaker, Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, Bulkhead, and Timeout — the four patterns that prevent individual failures from cascading across a distributed system.

section.name: circuit-breaker

rule.statement: Wrap every external I/O call (API, database, cache) in a circuit breaker with explicit failure threshold, open timeout, and success threshold.
rule.rationale: Without a circuit breaker, a slow downstream causes thread pool exhaustion in the caller, which then propagates to its callers (cascading failure). A circuit breaker fails fast once the threshold is reached.

section.p: Three states: Closed (normal) → Open (fail fast after threshold) → Half-Open (test recovery). Typical config: failure threshold 5-10 failures in a 10-60s window; open timeout 30-60s; success threshold 3-5 in half-open.

section.name: retry-with-exponential-backoff-and-jitter

rule.statement: Apply jitter to exponential backoff delays to prevent thundering herd when multiple callers retry simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Pure exponential backoff synchronises retries across many clients — all wait exactly 2s, then 4s — causing traffic spikes. Jitter spreads retry attempts across time.

rule.statement: Retry only on transient errors (5xx, timeouts, connection errors). Never retry 4xx client errors except 429 (rate limited).
rule.rationale: Retrying a 400 Bad Request wastes resources and masks a bug in the caller. Retrying 429 with the Retry-After header respects server capacity.

section.p: AWS/Netflix recommended decorrelated jitter formula: wait = min(cap, random(base, prev_sleep * 3)). Cap at 30-60s; max 3-5 attempts.

section.name: bulkhead

rule.statement: Assign separate thread pools or semaphores to each critical dependency so that a slow dependency cannot exhaust shared resources.
rule.rationale: A single thread pool shared by all downstream calls means one slow dependency starves all others. Bulkheads contain blast radius to a single partition.

section.p: Thread-pool bulkhead: full isolation, supports timeouts; higher overhead. Semaphore bulkhead: lower overhead, no timeout for waiting threads. Size each pool to expected concurrency, not max capacity.

rule.statement: Set explicit connect, read, and request timeouts on every external call; never use infinite timeouts.
rule.rationale: Infinite timeouts cause threads to block indefinitely during a downstream hang, eventually exhausting the thread pool silently.

rule.statement: Propagate a deadline budget across a call chain so each downstream call receives the remaining time, not the original full timeout.
rule.rationale: Without deadline propagation, a chain of 3 services each with a 10s timeout can block for 30s even though the upstream client has already given up at 5s.

antipattern.description: Applying retry without circuit breaker on a dependency that is completely down.
antipattern.reason: Retries amplify load on an already-failing service, accelerating its recovery delay. Circuit breaker must open first to stop retry traffic.

example.title: pattern-stack
example.note: Stack in order: Timeout → Circuit Breaker → Retry with jitter → Bulkhead. Each layer handles a different failure mode: Timeout prevents infinite wait; CB stops cascading; Retry handles transient; Bulkhead limits resource impact.

section.name: graceful-degradation
section.p: Classify features by criticality (P0-P3), define fallback behaviour at each tier, and implement load shedding to preserve core functionality when under stress.

rule.statement: Assign every feature a criticality tier (P0-P3) and define its degradation behaviour before a failure occurs, not during one.
rule.rationale: Incident response under pressure produces inconsistent decisions. Pre-classified features and documented fallbacks allow automated or rapid manual degradation without debate.

list.item: P0 (Critical): Never degrade; allocate maximum resources. Example: checkout, authentication.
P1 (Important): Degrade to cached or simplified response. Example: search, product pages.
P2 (Standard): Disable or show generic content. Example: personalised recommendations, reviews.
P3 (Optional): Silently disable. Example: analytics tracking, A/B test assignment.
Static Fallback: return cached or default data when live service unavailable (cached product catalogue)
Simplified Response: return basic subset of data, omit non-essential fields (basic user profile without preferences)
Feature Toggle: disable feature at runtime via flag without deployment (turn off recommendation engine)
Load Shedding: reject low-priority requests at admission to protect high-priority paths
Read-Only Mode: disable writes but allow reads when write path is degraded

example.title: p2-feature-degradation
example.note: On recommendation service failure: P2 feature → return popular products from in-process cache rather than 500 error. User sees generic list; checkout path (P0) is unaffected.

antipattern.description: Returning an error page for any downstream failure, including non-critical P2/P3 features.
antipattern.reason: Degrades the entire user experience for a failure in an optional feature. Partial page rendering with degraded components is always preferable for P2/P3.

section.name: load-shedding-priority

rule.statement: Implement admission control at the service entry point that rejects requests by priority tier as load increases.
rule.rationale: Without shedding, overload affects all tiers equally including P0. Prioritised shedding ensures P0 operations always have capacity.

section.p: Load shedding ladder: Normal → accept all. High load → reject P3. Critical load → reject P2+P3. Overload → accept only P0 + health checks. Assign request priority via headers or request attributes at the gateway.

section.name: health-checks-disaster-recovery-and-chaos-engineering
section.p: Kubernetes probe design, DR strategy selection by RTO/RPO, 3-2-1 backup rule, and the chaos engineering maturity model for validating reliability assumptions.

rule.statement: Liveness probe must check only the process itself (no external deps); readiness probe must check all critical dependencies.
rule.rationale: If liveness checks the database and the database is slow, Kubernetes kills and restarts the pod — which does not fix the database. Liveness should only detect deadlocks and infinite loops. Readiness correctly removes the pod from load balancer rotation when deps are unavailable.

rule.statement: Use a startup probe for slow-starting applications with failureThreshold * periodSeconds greater than the maximum startup time.
rule.rationale: Without startup probe, liveness begins immediately and kills the pod before it finishes initialising — causing a restart loop.

list.item: GET /health/live → liveness: 200 OK if process running, no external checks
GET /health/ready → readiness: checks DB, cache, critical external APIs; returns 503 if any critical dependency unhealthy
GET /health/startup → startup: verifies essential initialisation complete
GET /health/detailed → internal only (not exposed publicly); full dependency status with latencies

rule.statement: Select a DR strategy based on explicit RTO and RPO requirements, not by default to the cheapest option.
rule.rationale: Backup/Restore is cheap but has hours of RTO — unacceptable for a payment API with a 15-minute SLA. Matching strategy to requirement avoids both over-spending and under-protecting.

list.item: Backup/Restore: RTO hours, RPO hours, cost $. Use for non-critical workloads.
Pilot Light: RTO 10-30 min, RPO minutes, cost $$. Core services warm, scale on failure.
Warm Standby: RTO minutes, RPO seconds, cost $$$. Scaled-down copy always running.
Hot Standby / Multi-Active: RTO seconds/zero, RPO near-zero, cost $$$$. Mission-critical only.

rule.statement: Apply the 3-2-1 backup rule: 3 copies, 2 different storage media types, 1 offsite location.
rule.rationale: A single backup in the same region as production is destroyed by the same event (region outage, ransomware) that caused the original failure.

section.name: chaos-engineering

rule.statement: Define steady-state hypothesis before each chaos experiment; abort if SLO is breached during the experiment.
rule.rationale: Without a hypothesis, chaos is destructive testing not learning. Aborting on SLO breach limits blast radius and preserves the error budget.

section.p: Maturity levels: 1=Manual failover game days, 2=Automated chaos in staging, 3=Production chaos at low traffic, 4=Continuous always-on chaos. Progress sequentially — never start at level 3 without level 2 confidence.

list.item: Instance termination: tests auto-scaling and failover
Network latency injection (200ms): tests timeout and retry behaviour
Network partition: tests split-brain handling
CPU/memory stress: tests autoscaling and throttling
Zone/region failure: tests multi-zone/multi-region resilience

template.purpose: SLO document template with SLI expression, target, window, and escalation thresholds
Python circuit breaker implementation with decorator usage
tenacity-based retry decorator factory with jitter
FastAPI liveness/readiness/startup endpoints with structured response
